structured_payload_candidate.title: Control Loop Enable Based on System Status
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Control loops shall be enabled and disabled based on the status of the system being controlled to prevent windup.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Unless otherwise indicated, control loops shall be enabled and disabled based on the status of the system being controlled to prevent windup.

evidence_quote: Unless otherwise indicated, control loops shall be enabled and disabled based on the status of the system being controlled to prevent windup.
evidence_text: Unless otherwise indicated, control loops shall be enabled and disabled based on the status of the system being controlled to prevent windup.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Control Loop Initialization to Neutral
structured_payload_candidate.summary: When a control loop is enabled or re-enabled, all constituents (proportional, integral terms) shall be set initially to a neutral value.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: set all loop constituents to neutral
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: When a control loop is enabled or reenabled, it and all its constituents (such as the proportional and integral terms) shall be set initially to a neutral value.

evidence_quote: When a control loop is enabled or reenabled, it and all its constituents (such as the proportional and integral terms) shall be set initially to a neutral value.
evidence_text: When a control loop is enabled or reenabled, it and all its constituents (such as the proportional and integral terms) shall be set initially to a neutral value.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Outdoor Air Temperature Sensor Validity at AHU Intake
structured_payload_candidate.summary: An outdoor air temperature sensor at an AHU intake is considered valid only when the supply fan is proven ON and the unit is in Occupied Mode or any mode with the economizer enabled.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: supply fan status and operating mode
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: treat sensor as valid only under stated conditions
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Outdoor air temperature sensors at air-handler outdoor air intakes shall be considered valid only when the supply fan is proven ON and the unit is in Occupied Mode or in any other mode with the economizer enabled.

evidence_quote: Outdoor air temperature sensors at air-handler outdoor air intakes shall be considered valid only when the supply fan is proven ON and the unit is in Occupied Mode or in any other mode with the economizer enabled.
evidence_text: Outdoor air temperature sensors at air-handler outdoor air intakes shall be considered valid only when the supply fan is proven ON and the unit is in Occupied Mode or in any other mode with the economizer enabled.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Global Outdoor Air Temperature Calculation
structured_payload_candidate.summary: The outdoor air temperature used for global sequences shall be the average of all valid sensor readings; if four or more valid sensors exist, discard the highest and lowest readings.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: computation of global outdoor air temperature
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: compute average of valid sensors, discarding extremes when ≥4 sensors
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: The outdoor air temperature used for optimum start, plant lockout, and other global sequences shall be the average of all valid sensor readings. If there are four or more valid outdoor air temperature sensors, discard the highest and lowest temperature readings.

evidence_quote: The outdoor air temperature used for optimum start, plant lockout, and other global sequences shall be the average of all valid sensor readings. If there are four or more valid outdoor air temperature sensors, discard the highest and lowest temperature readings.
evidence_text: The outdoor air temperature used for optimum start, plant lockout, and other global sequences shall be the average of all valid sensor readings. If there are four or more valid outdoor air temperature sensors, discard the highest and lowest temperature readings.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Control Loop Output Rate-of-Change Limit
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Every control loop output shall be capable of being limited by a user-adjustable maximum rate of change, with a default of 25% per minute.
configurable_values: user-adjustable maximum rate of change, default 25% per minute
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: To avoid abrupt changes in equipment operation, the output of every control loop shall be capable of being limited by a user adjustable maximum rate of change, with a default of 25% per minute.

evidence_quote: To avoid abrupt changes in equipment operation, the output of every control loop shall be capable of being limited by a user adjustable maximum rate of change, with a default of 25% per minute.
evidence_text: To avoid abrupt changes in equipment operation, the output of every control loop shall be capable of being limited by a user adjustable maximum rate of change, with a default of 25% per minute.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Alarm Levels Definition
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Four alarm levels are defined: Level 1 (life-safety), Level 2 (critical equipment), Level 3 (urgent), Level 4 (normal).
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: There shall be 4 levels of alarm a. Level 1: Life-safety message b. Level 2: Critical equipment message c. Level 3: Urgent message d. Level 4: Normal message

structured_payload_candidate.title: Maintenance Mode Alarm Suppression
structured_payload_candidate.summary: When a device is placed in maintenance mode, all associated alarms are suppressed except life-safety alarms; a daily Level 3 alarm is generated to remind that the device is in maintenance mode.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: suppress alarms (except life-safety), generate daily Level 3 reminder
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Maintenance Mode. Operators shall have the ability to put any device (e.g., AHU) in/out of maintenance mode. a. All alarms associated with a device in maintenance mode will be suppressed. Exception: Life safety alarms shall not be suppressed. b. If a device is in maintenance mode, issue a daily Level 3 alarm at a scheduled time indicating that the device is still in maintenance mode.

structured_payload_candidate.title: VFD Minimum Speed Mismatch Detection
structured_payload_candidate.section_title: VFD Speed Points: Minimum Speed Monitoring
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Every 60 minutes, read the VFD's active minimum speed via network. If it differs from the software-point minimum speed, write the software minimum speed back to the VFD and generate a Level 4 alarm.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: mismatch between software minimum speed and VFD active minimum speed
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: overwrite VFD minimum speed with software value, generate Level 4 alarm
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: The active minimum speed parameter shall be read every 60 minutes via the drive’s network interface. When a mismatch between the drive’s active minimum speed and the minimum speed stored in the software point is detected, the minimum speed stored in the software point shall be written to the VFD via the network interface to restore the active minimum speed parameter to its default value, and generate a Level 4 alarm.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Trim & Respond Setpoint Reset Logic
structured_payload_candidate.equipment_scope: upstream system/plant setpoint controlled by T&R loop
structured_payload_candidate.summary: When the associated device is proven ON (after delay Td), every time step T: if R ≤ I, trim setpoint by SPtrim; if R > I, change setpoint by (R – I)*SPres, capped at SPres-max. Setpoint is bounded between SPmin and SPmax; when device is OFF, setpoint = SP0.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: cycle T&R adjustment every T, respond to requests as described
configurable_values: SP0, SPmin, SPmax, Td, T, I, SPtrim, SPres, SPres-max (as defined in Table 5.1.14.3)
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Trim & Respond logic shall reset the setpoint within the range SPmin to SPmax. When the associated device is OFF, the setpoint shall be SP0. The reset logic shall be active while the associated device is proven ON, starting Td after initial device start command. When active, every time step T, if R≤I, trim the setpoint by SPtrim. If there are more than I requests, respond by changing the setpoint by SPres *(R – I), (i.e., the number of requests minus the number of ignored requests) but no more than SPres-max.

evidence_quote: See Section 5.1.14.4  for an example of T&R implementation.
anchor_repair_reason: evidence_quote replaced with verbatim section heading line
evidence_text: See Section 5.1.14.4  for an example of T&R implementation.

structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: multiply requests by Importance-Multiplier, send upstream
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Multiply the number of requests determined from zone/system logic times the Importance-Multiplier and send to the system/plant that serves the zone/system.

evidence_quote: Multiply the number of requests determined from zone/system logic times the Importance-Multiplier and send to the system/plant that serves the zone/system.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Rogue Zone Detection via T&R Request Hours
structured_payload_candidate.equipment_scope: zone or downstream system in T&R loop
structured_payload_candidate.summary: A Level 4 alarm is generated if the zone Importance-Multiplier > 0, Cumulative% Request-Hours exceeds 70%, and zone/system run hours exceed 40.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: Importance-Multiplier > 0 and Cumulative% Request-Hours > 70% and run hours > 40

evidence_quote: A Level 4 alarm is generated if the zone Importance-Multiplier is greater than zero, the zone/system Cumulative% Request Hours exceeds 70%, and the total number of zone/system run hours exceeds 40.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Parallel Equipment Rotation Requirement
structured_payload_candidate.equipment_scope: parallel equipment (pumps, fans, chillers, boilers, etc.)
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Parallel equipment shall be lead/lag or lead/standby rotated to maintain even wear.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Parallel equipment shall be lead/lag or lead/standby rotated to maintain even wear.

evidence_quote: Parallel equipment shall be lead/lag or lead/standby rotated to maintain even wear.
evidence_text: Parallel equipment shall be lead/lag or lead/standby rotated to maintain even wear.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Lead/Lag Equipment Rotation
structured_payload_candidate.equipment_scope: identical parallel staged equipment (e.g., CHW pumps, cooling towers)
structured_payload_candidate.summary: When more than one is off or more than one is on, alternate such that equipment with the most Staging Runtime hours is made the last stage, and the one with the least hours is made the lead stage.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: staging order update when more than one off or more than one on
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: assign lead based on least runtime, lag based on most runtime
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Unless otherwise noted, identical parallel staged equipment (such as CHW pumps and cooling towers) shall be lead/lag alternated when more than one is off or more than one is on so that the equipment with the most operating hours as determined by Staging Runtime is made the last stage equipment and the one with the least number of hours is made the lead stage equipment.

evidence_quote: Unless otherwise noted, identical parallel staged equipment (such as CHW pumps and cooling towers) shall be lead/lag alternated when more than one is off or more than one is on so that the equipment with the most operating hours as determined by Staging Runtime is made the last stage equipment and the one with the least number of hours is made the lead stage equipment.
evidence_text: Unless otherwise noted, identical parallel staged equipment (such as CHW pumps and cooling towers) shall be lead/lag alternated when more than one is off or more than one is on so that the equipment with the most operating hours as determined by Staging Runtime is made the last stage equipment and the one with the least number of hours is made the lead stage equipment.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Lead/Standby Rotation When Equipment Off
structured_payload_candidate.summary: When more than one of the equipment is off, alternate so that equipment with the most Staging Runtime hours is made the last stage and the one with the least hours is made the earlier stage.
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Unless equipment runs continuously, parallel equipment that are 100% redundant shall be lead/standby alternated when more than one of the equipment is off so that the equipment with the most operating hours as determined by Staging Runtime is made the last stage equipment and the one with the least number of hours is made the earlier stage equipment.

evidence_quote: Unless equipment runs continuously, parallel equipment that are 100% redundant shall be lead/standby alternated when more than one of the equipment is off so that the equipment with the most operating hours as determined by Staging Runtime is made the last stage equipment and the one with the least number of hours is made the earlier stage equipment.
evidence_text: Unless equipment runs continuously, parallel equipment that are 100% redundant shall be lead/standby alternated when more than one of the equipment is off so that the equipment with the most operating hours as determined by Staging Runtime is made the last stage equipment and the one with the least number of hours is made the earlier stage equipment.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Lead/Standby Rotation for Continuously Running Equipment
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Lead/standby positions shall switch at an adjustable day/time (e.g., every Tuesday 10:00 am) based on Staging Runtime; standby equipment shall first be started and proven on before former lead is changed to standby and shut off.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: start standby, prove on, then switch lead to standby and shut off former lead
configurable_values: adjustable day of the week and time
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: If equipment runs continuously, lead/standby positions shall switch at an adjustable day of the week and time (e.g., every Tuesday at 10:00 am) based on Staging Runtime; standby equipment shall first be started and proven on before former lead equipment is changed to standby and shut off.

evidence_quote: If equipment runs continuously, lead/standby positions shall switch at an adjustable day of the week and time (e.g., every Tuesday at 10:00 am) based on Staging Runtime; standby equipment shall first be started and proven on before former lead equipment is changed to standby and shut off.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Fan/Pump Fault Detection
structured_payload_candidate.summary: If the status point does not match its on/off point for 3 seconds after a time delay of 15 seconds while commanded ON, a Level 2 alarm is generated.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Chiller Fault Conditions
structured_payload_candidate.summary: A chiller is faulted if any of these occur: safety shutdown alarm; manually shut off (Local/Auto switch); status remains off 5 min after start command; CHW isolation valve not open 90 sec after command; CHW isolation valve not closed 90 sec after command (series chillers); CW isolation valve not open 90 sec after command; or for 10 minutes, CHWRT ≥ CHWST setpoint + 3°C (5°F) and chiller ΔT < 2°C (3°F).
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: set equipment as faulted, generate Level 2 alarm
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: ii. Chillers (a) Safety shutdown alarm condition either through network or hardwired alarm contact (b) Chiller is manually shut off as indicated by the status of the Local/Auto switch from chiller gateway, or (c) Chiller status remains off 5 minutes after command to start (note: this condition only applies when a chiller first starts, i.e., once status is proven, then status is no longer used as a fault condition because status will come and go if chiller cycles on low load), or Retain the following sentence for plants with parallel chillers and CHW isolation valve position feedback. Delete otherwise. (d) CHW isolation valve feedback indicates valve is not open 90 seconds after valve is commanded open, or Retain the following sentence for plants with series chillers and CHW isolation valve position feedback. Delete otherwise. (e) CHW isolation valve feedback indicates valve is not closed 90 seconds after valve is commanded closed, or Retain the following sentence for chillers with CW isolation valve position feedback. Delete otherwise. (f) CW isolation valve feedback indicates valve is not open 90 seconds after valve is commanded open, or (g) For 10 minutes, chilled water return temperature has been at least 3°C (5°F) above the CHWST setpoint, and delta-T across the chiller, as determined based on the difference between chilled water return temperature and chilled water supply temperature measured at the chiller (i.e., not common CHWST), has been less than 2°C (3°F).

structured_payload_candidate.title: SystemOK FALSE Conditions
structured_payload_candidate.summary: SystemOK shall be FALSE during startup (before reaching setpoint) or when enough components are unavailable (alarm/disabled/OFF) to disrupt load serving. By default, Level 1–3 component alarms inhibit SystemOK, Level 4 do not. Operator can selectively override which alarms inhibit SystemOK.
structured_payload_candidate.trigger_condition: system startup or insufficient available components
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: SystemOK shall be FALSE while the system is starting up (i.e., before reaching setpoint) or when enough of the system’s components are unavailable (in alarm, disabled, or turned OFF) to disrupt the ability of the system to serve its load. ... a. By default, Level 1 through Level 3 component alarms (indicating equipment failure) shall inhibit SystemOK. Level 4 component alarms (maintenance and energy efficiency alarms) shall not affect SystemOK. b. The operator shall have the ability to individually determine which component alarms may or may not inhibit SystemOK.

structured_payload_candidate.title: Hierarchical Alarm Suppression Rules
structured_payload_candidate.summary: If SystemOK is FALSE for a source system: for cooling water system, suppress high-temp alarms from loads; for heating water system, suppress low-temp alarms from loads; for air-side system, suppress all alarms from loads. Suppression cascades downstream. Life/safety, failure-to-start, and failure-to-stop alarms are never suppressed.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: selectively suppress load alarms as described
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: The BAS shall selectively suppress (i.e., fail to announce; alarms may still be logged to a database) alarms for load components if SystemOK is FALSE for the source system that serves that load. a. If SystemOK is FALSE for a cooling water system (i.e., chiller, cooling tower, or associated pump), then only high-temperature alarms from the loads shall be suppressed. b. If SystemOK is FALSE for a heating water system (i.e., boiler or associated pump), then only low temperature alarms from the loads shall be suppressed. c. If SystemOK is FALSE for an air-side system (air handler, fan coil, VAV box, etc.), then all alarms from the loads shall be suppressed. ... The following types of alarms will never be suppressed by this logic: a. Life/safety and Level 1 alarms b. Failure-to-start alarms (i.e., equipment is commanded ON, but status point shows equipment to be OFF) c. Failure-to-stop/hand alarms (i.e., equipment is commanded OFF, but status point shows equipment to be ON)

structured_payload_candidate.title: Time-Based Alarm Suppression Default Delay
structured_payload_candidate.summary: Calculate a time-delay period after any setpoint change based on the difference between the controlled variable and the new setpoint. Default: 18 minutes per °C (10 min per °F), up to 120 minutes max.
structured_payload_candidate.required_behavior: suppress alarms/reset requests for calculated delay
configurable_values: default delay 18 min/°C (10 min/°F), max 120 min
structured_payload_candidate.evidence_quote: Calculate a time-delay period after any change in setpoint based on the difference between the controlled variable (e.g., zone temperature) at the time of the change and the new setpoint. The default time delay period shall be as follows: a. For thermal zone temperature alarms: 18 minutes per °C (10 minutes per °F) of difference but no longer than 120 minutes
